title: The 1X Neo Robot Has Freaky Fast Fingers
gist: Norwegian-American robotics firm 1X detailed the five-finger hands on its soft, Baymax-inspired home robot Neo (25 degrees of freedom vs. 27 for humans), pricing it at $20,000 or $500/month, but the article flags that Neo is still partly teleoperated via 'Expert Mode'—meaning human operators can see inside buyers' homes—and that 1X's demo videos mix machine-articulated and human-operated footage, obscuring true autonomous capability.

insights.claim: Neo's hands use tendon-mimicking actuators giving 25 degrees of freedom, just under the 27 of human hands, and can grip odd shapes, detect slipping, hyperextend beyond human range, and carry an IP68 waterproof rating.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Near-human dexterity is the gating capability for useful home humanoids; specifics like slip detection and IP68 signal 1X is targeting real daily-task utility, not just demos.
insights.quote: 1X says this gives Neo's hands 25 degrees of freedom of movement, which is just a little less than the 27 degrees of freedom human hands usually have.
insights.locator: The hands are built with actuators

insights.claim: 1X is deliberately positioning against defense-oriented 'Terminator' humanoids with a soft, supple Baymax-inspired robot wrapped in a 3D lattice shell, betting that consumer comfort is a differentiator.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: A consumer-comfort-first design targets the home market that hulking industrial/defense humanoids ignore—a distinct positioning and TAM bet within humanoid robotics.
insights.quote: The humanoid robot market tends to be dominated by tough, hulking Terminators meant to be paraded out for some defense industry contract or another. 1X is taking a different tack
insights.locator: The humanoid robot market tends to be dominated

insights.claim: Neo is priced at $20,000 outright or $500 per month for the home in limited quantities, with the lump-sum option prioritizing delivery in 2026.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: A concrete consumer price and subscription option set the willingness-to-pay benchmark for home humanoids and a 2026 delivery milestone to track.
insights.quote: early access pricing is $20,000 or $500 per month to have it in your home, though the lump sum will prioritize delivery for 2026
insights.locator: In limited quantities, early access pricing

insights.claim: The launch is framed as part of a 'ChatGPT-esque moment for robotics,' with bots moving from clumsy claw machines to capable handlers of fragile objects and menial tasks.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: The 'ChatGPT moment' framing captures the inflection thesis driving robotics capital—useful narrative to test against actual shipped autonomy.
insights.quote: this is part of a growing ChatGPT-esque moment for robotics, where bots have gone from clumsy claw machines to capable handlers
insights.locator: Together, this is part of a growing

insights.claim: Neo is not fully autonomous; it is partly teleoperated via a feature called Expert Mode where human operators remotely control it and view your home through its camera to handle complex chores.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Teleoperation is the tell that current home humanoids aren't truly autonomous—labor cost and scalability, not just hardware, are the real constraints.
insights.quote: for now, the robot is partly teleoperated, as a Wall Street Journal video noted last year. That means human operators can take control of Neo remotely and even look through a camera
insights.locator: 1X says the goal is full automation

insights.claim: The teleoperation model creates a privacy attack surface: a human-operated robot in intimate home settings, and 1X did not immediately answer how it will prevent bad actors or hackers from taking over the robot.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: Unresolved remote-takeover and surveillance risk is a material adoption and liability barrier for in-home robots that could invite regulation.
insights.quote: 1X did not immediately answer questions about how the company plans to prevent bad actors or hackers from taking over the robot.
insights.locator: 1X says that human "experts" can only ever enter

insights.claim: 1X's marketing obscures true capability: a representative said 'some of the videos are machine-articulated while some are operated to show the upper limit of the hardware capabilities,' so it is not yet possible to tell how well the actuators actually work autonomously.
insights.speaker: Boone Ashworth
insights.so_what: When demo footage blends autonomous and teleoperated clips, capability claims should be discounted—a due-diligence red flag common across the humanoid sector.
insights.quote: some of the videos are machine-articulated while some are operated to show the upper limit of the hardware capabilities
insights.locator: The other problem with the human element
